retrieved_docs.content: Ductal perforation Vascular injury Complications related to anesthesia Postoperative ductal stenosis

retrieved_docs.title: Benign Salivary Gland Tumors -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Surgical excision is the standard treatment for all salivary gland tumors. This may lead to a loss of functional tissue and saliva secretion, causing xerostomia, dental caries, halitosis, periodontal disease, and oral infections. [107] The incidence of temporary facial nerve palsy after parotidectomy ranges from 10% to 65%, with permanent paralysis seen in less than 5%. [128] [129] [130] The incidence of Frey syndrome after parotidectomy varies widely from 2% to 80% due to the time interval since surgery as well as the surveillance criteria from the surgeons. [131] Treatments for Frey syndrome have included antiperspirant ointment, botulinum toxin A injections, and barrier flaps such as superficial musculoaponeurotic system (SMAS) flap, temporoparietal flap, sternocleidomastoid flap, anterolateral thigh flap, or thick skin flap. [131] [132] [133] [134] Additional complications from surgical resection include sialocele, salivary fistula, neuromas of the great auricular nerve, and preauricular skin anesthesia. [51] Patients who undergo submandibular gland resection have a risk of neurological damage to the hypoglossal nerve, lingual nerve, and marginal mandibular branch of the facial nerve. [7] [135] [7] Complications from radiation include sensorineural hearing loss, chronic otitis media/externa, otalgia, skin erythema, mucositis, dysphagia, dysgeusia, xerostomia, soft tissue fibrosis, osteoradionecrosis, and radiation-induced malignancy. [94] [136] Approximately 36% of patients developed hearing loss of 10 dB and higher at 4kHz. [137] Mandibular osteoradionecrosis (<2%) and RT-induced malignancy (1%) at 10 to 25 years are rare complications. [136] [138] [139] [140]

retrieved_docs.content: Hemorrhage Wharton duct damage leading to stenosis, and obstructive sialadenitis Injury to the lingual nerve causing temporary or permanent paresthesia Facial nerve marginal mandibular branch damage-causing paresthesia

retrieved_docs.content: Anti–Sjögren-syndrome-related antigen A autoantibodies, SSB/anti-La, antinuclear antibody, rheumatoid factor tests: If sialadenitis secondary to connective tissue disorders is suspected.

retrieved_docs.title: Submandibular Sialadenitis and Sialadenosis -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the clinical signs and symptoms of submandibular sialadenitis and sialadenosis. Differentiate between inflammatory submandibular sialadenitis and non-inflammatory sialadenosis based on clinical presentation and patient history. Implement conservative management strategies to treat early-stage submandibular sialadenitis and prevent progression. Communicate clear and accurate information across the interprofessional healthcare team to coordinate patient care, including shared findings from imaging, pathology, and clinical evaluations. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Malignant Salivary Gland Tumors -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: The incidence of temporary facial nerve palsy after parotidectomy ranges from 10% to 65%, with permanent paralysis seen in less than 5%. [146] [220] [221] The incidence of Frey syndrome after parotidectomy varies widely from 2% to 80% due to the time interval since surgery as well as the surveillance criteria from the surgeons. [222] Treatment for Frey syndrome includes antiperspirant ointment, botulinum toxin A injections, and barrier flaps such as superficial musculoaponeurotic system flap, temporoparietal flap, sternocleidomastoid flap, anterolateral thigh flap, or thick skin flaps. [222] [223] [224] [225] Additional complications from surgical resection include sialocele, salivary fistula, neuromas of the great auricular nerve, and preauricular skin anesthesia. [113] Patients who undergo submandibular gland resection or neck dissection for cervical lymph node metastasis have a risk of neurological damage to the spinal accessory, phrenic, hypoglossal, lingual, vagus, sympathetic trunk, and the marginal mandibular branch of the facial nerve. [226] [227] [228] Complications from radiation include sensorineural hearing loss, chronic otitis media/externa, otalgia, skin erythema, mucositis, dysphagia, dysgeusia, xerostomia, soft tissue fibrosis, osteoradionecrosis, and radiation-induced malignancy. [34] [113] In a study, approximately 36% of patients were found to develop hearing loss of 10 dB or higher at 4kHz. [229] Mandibular osteoradionecrosis (<2%) and radiation-induced malignancy (1%) at 10 to 25 years are rare complications. [113] [230] [231] [232]

retrieved_docs.title: Submandibular Salivary Gland Excision -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Assess patients with submandibular gland disease for indications and contraindications to submandibular gland excision. Determine the most suitable approach to submandibular gland excision based on pathology, patient factors, and surgical considerations. Implement evidence-based, personalized strategies for managing common and rare complications of submandibular gland excision. Collaborate with all members of the interprofessional team, including primary and emergency care providers, oncologists, otolaryngologists, anesthesiologists, and nursing staff, to provide efficient, comprehensive, and coordinated care for individuals with submandibular gland disease. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Submandibular Sialadenitis and Sialadenosis -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: In this course, participants learn to identify the etiology, clinical features, and management strategies for both submandibular sialadenitis and sialadenosis. The course emphasizes the importance of early diagnosis and conservative management to improve patient outcomes. Collaboration with an interprofessional team, including otolaryngologists, dentists, and radiologists, is highlighted, which enhances care by ensuring comprehensive evaluation, accurate diagnosis, and coordinated treatment, ultimately reducing complications and improving long-term patient quality of life.

retrieved_docs.title: Submandibular Sialadenitis and Sialadenosis -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The exact prevalence of submandibular sialadenitis is not clear. Submandibular gland sialadenitis accounts for about 10% of all cases of sialadenitis and accounts for about 0.001% to 0.002% of all hospital admissions. There is no age or sex predilection for this condition; however, it commonly affects patients who are older or dehydrated. Sialadenosis is the most common cause of salivary gland swelling in the United States.

retrieved_docs.title: Submandibular Sialadenitis and Sialadenosis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Complete blood count to rule out infections

retrieved_docs.title: Submandibular Sialadenitis and Sialadenosis -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: In acute suppurative submandibular sialadenitis, interstitial neutrophilic infiltration and necrosis with acinar destruction are present. Vacuolar changes in the acini cells and lymphocytic infiltration are more common in viral sialadenitis. Dilated duct with calculi, chronic inflammation, and fibrosis are evident in chronic sialadenitis due to sialolith. In sialadenosis, there is atrophy of parenchymal tissue and a compensatory increase in the amount of adipose tissue. Inflammatory infiltrates are absent.

retrieved_docs.title: Sialolithiasis -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Sialolithiasis is the most common benign cause of salivary gland swelling; however, it remains a relatively rare diagnosis with an incidence of 1 in 10,000 to 1 in 30,000. The disease commonly gets diagnosed in the primary care or emergency medicine setting, where initial patient contact is mostly with nurses, nurse practitioners, physician assistants, primary care physicians, and emergency medicine physicians. Given the oral nature of the disease, some patients may initially present to their dentist for evaluation of symptoms. Diagnostic radiologists are often involved in the initial workup for imaging recommendations and interpretation. The potential involvement of all these disciplines is why an interprofessional team approach is necessary for the diagnosis and management of this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Mumps (Nursing) -- Assessment
retrieved_docs.content: The prodrome of the mumps virus includes nonspecific symptoms such as fever, malaise, headache, myalgias, and anorexia shortly followed by parotitis in the following days. Mumps parotitis is the most common manifestation of the virus occurring in over 70% of infections. Parotid swelling is usually bilateral, but unilateral swelling can occur [1] . Parotid swelling presents as painful inflammation of the area between the earlobe and the angle of the mandible. The mucosa of Stenson’s duct is often red and swollen along with the involvement of the submaxillary and submandibular glands. [6] [7] Glandular inflammation most often presents but then subsides within one week. [8] Recurrent sialadenitis is a frequent complication of parotitis. Mumps during pregnancy is unknown to lead to premature birth, low birth weight, or fetal malformation. [1] [9] [10] [11] [12] Orchitis is the next most common manifestation of mumps which leads to painful swelling, enlargement, and tenderness of the testes which is most often bilateral. Testicular atrophy develops in one half of those affected. Sterility and subfertility after mumps infection is rare and occurs in less than 15% of cases. [1] [7] [13] [14] [15] Oophoritis is also rare amongst infected females with less than 5% developing infertility or pre-menopause. [1] Neurological manifestations include meningitis, encephalitis, transverse myelitis, Guillan-Bare syndrome, cerebellar ataxia, facial palsy, and hydrocephalus. Neurological complications are typically self-resolving, and there is a low incidence of morbidity and mortality. [16] Additional systemic rare complications include pancreatitis, myocarditis, thyroiditis, nephritis, hepatic disease, arthritis, keratitis, and thrombocytopenic purpura. [17] [18] [19] [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Parotitis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Viral or bacterial infections are typically the primary cause of acute parotitis. Common viral pathogens include mumps, influenza, parainfluenza, coxsackievirus, echovirus, and HIV. Staphylococcus aureus and Streptococcus species are the most common bacterial causes. [1] Additional causes of acute parotitis are mechanical obstruction from salivary stones or ductal strictures, external beam radiation, contrast exposure, and radioiodine treatment. Chronic parotitis often results from recurrent infections; autoimmune conditions, such as Sjögren disease or sarcoidosis; tumors; and metabolic disorders, such as diabetes mellitus. Additional risk factors include tuberculosis, inflammatory disorders, dehydration, malnutrition, immunosuppression, and medications that reduce salivary flow. Although uncommon, complications may include facial nerve palsy, sepsis, Lemierre syndrome, osteomyelitis, and multiorgan failure.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 72
retrieved_docs.content: The category “diseases of mouth; excluding dental” is classified as a subcategory of “اِضطِراب الغدة اللُّعابِيَّة” (salivary gland disorder), indicating that non‑dental oral diseases are considered part of the broader spectrum of salivary gland pathology. This relationship highlights the importance of evaluating salivary gland function when assessing oral conditions that are not of dental origin, thereby guiding more comprehensive diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2181
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is classified as a malignant tumor in the sublingual gland (ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان), indicating that this metastatic cancer represents a specific form of malignant neoplasm affecting the sublingual salivary gland. Recognizing this relationship clarifies the pathological nature and precise anatomical location of the disease, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic planning in patients with salivary gland malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 0 -- Community 25
retrieved_docs.content: is contraindicated in pancreatitis, and that pancreatic disorders unrelated to diabetes are mapped to pancreatitis. Pancreatitis is associated with increased systemic and tissue alkalinity, a factor that may affect diagnostic interpretation and therapeutic choices.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1970
retrieved_docs.content: The category “DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES” is positioned within the broader group of “other endocrine disorders” and encompasses a range of specific pancreatic and metabolic conditions. Among its sub‑entities are “مرض السكري دون مضاعفات” (diabetes without complications), “نقص تركيز السكر” (hypoglycemia), “اعتلال عصبي سكري” (diabetic neuropathy), “ضمور نصف الوجه المترقي” (progressive hemifacial atrophy), “فرط أنسولين الدم” (hyperinsulinemia), and “ورم غلوكاغوني” (glucagonoma). Hypoglycemia is therefore recognized both as a distinct manifestation under the diabetes‑related pancreatic disease spectrum and as a condition that belongs to the larger classification of other endocrine disorders. Collectively, these entities illustrate the diverse clinical presentations that can arise from endocrine dysfunction of the pancreas, highlighting the importance of recognizing both isolated metabolic disturbances and their broader endocrine context for accurate diagnosis and comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 193
retrieved_docs.content: of the sublingual gland). Carcinoma of the head of the pancreas is morphologically associated with الورم السرطاني (cancerous tumor) and is mapped to the broader term cancer of pancreas, indicating that this anatomic variant is a subset of pancreatic cancer. Metastatic involvement of lymph nodes in the head and neck region is captured by several entities: metastatic carcinoma to lymph node of the head and neck, metastatic adenocarcinoma to lymph node of the head and neck, and metastatic squamous cell carcinoma of lymph nodes of the head and neck. All three are classified as secondary malignancies, each “mapped to” the secondary‑malignancy group and described by inverse‑is‑a relationships to الورم السرطاني (cancerous tumor) or, for the squamous cell type, to سرطان الخلايا الحرشفية في الرأس والعنق (squamous cell carcinoma of the head and neck). Morphologically, metastatic adenocarcinoma to a head‑and‑neck lymph node is associated with سرطانة غدية نقيلية (metastatic glandular carcinoma),

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 189
retrieved_docs.content: each condition as a potential adverse event, enabling systematic risk assessment, targeted monitoring, operative decision‑making, and coordinated maternal‑fetal and newborn care aimed at reducing morbidity and improving outcomes for both mother and infant.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2709
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is identified as a specific form of malignant tumor in the sublingual gland, as indicated by its inverse_isa relationship to ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان. Both Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland and the broader category Neoplasm, uncertain whether benign or malignant share the relationship termed النقيلة, suggesting a common classification or characteristic within neoplastic disease. Recognizing that the metastatic malignant neoplasm to the sublingual gland represents a subset of malignant sublingual gland tumors underscores its aggressive potential and the need for thorough evaluation, while the ambiguous neoplasm category highlights the importance of histopathological assessment to determine benign versus malignant status. The linking term النقيلة therefore serves as a unifying descriptor that aids clinicians in diagnosing, categorizing, and managing these related neoplastic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 796
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is identified as a malignant tumor in the sublingual gland (ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان) and is linked through a “has relationship” connection to the term النقيلة. The entity Neoplasm, uncertain whether benign or malignant also has a “has relationship” connection to النقيلة, indicating that the concept النقيلة encompasses both clearly metastatic lesions and neoplasms of indeterminate behavior. Furthermore, النقيلة is related by “has relationship” to سرطان نقيلي في رأس البنكرياس (metastatic cancer in the head of the pancreas), suggesting that النقيلة functions as a unifying descriptor for metastatic disease across different anatomical sites. Together, these relationships illustrate that النقيلة serves as a common link among various malignant and uncertain neoplasms, including those arising in the sublingual gland and the pancreatic head, highlighting the importance of recognizing metastatic potential in diverse tumor types for accurate diagnosis and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1756
retrieved_docs.content: The category “DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES” encompasses a spectrum of disorders and clinical manifestations that arise from dysfunction of the pancreatic endocrine system. It includes “ارتفاع السكر في الدم” (hyperglycemia), which represents elevated blood‑glucose levels and is a primary feature of diabetes. “غيبوبة فرط الأسمولية السكري” (diabetic hyperosmolar coma) is a severe, acute complication of uncontrolled hyperglycemia characterized by extreme dehydration and altered mental status. “مرض السكري دون مضاعفات” (diabetes mellitus without complications) denotes diabetes in its uncomplicated form, serving as the baseline condition from which other sequelae may develop. Among the chronic complications, “اعتلال عصبي سكري” (diabetic neuropathy) reflects nerve damage caused by prolonged hyperglycemia, leading to sensory and motor deficits. “ضمور نصف الوجه المترقي” (progressive hemifacial atrophy) is listed as a possible extra‑pancreatic manifestation that can be associated with underlying endocrine pancreatic disease. Metabolic dysregulation is further illustrated by “فرط أنسولين الدم” (hyperinsulinemia), an excess of circulating insulin that may precede or accompany insulin resistance. Finally, “ورم غلوكاغوني” (glucagonoma) represents a rare pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor that secretes excess glucagon, producing a distinct clinical syndrome. Together, these entities illustrate the diverse acute, chronic, and neoplastic conditions linked to diseases of the endocrine pancreas, highlighting the importance of comprehensive assessment and management of both glycemic control and associated complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 93
retrieved_docs.content: abnormalities, treatment‑related complications, and a wide array of multilingual disease entities into a comprehensive classification system that supports accurate diagnosis, coding, reporting, and patient management across diverse medical domains.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1198
retrieved_docs.content: Nasogastric tube procedures classify the nasogastric tube, indicating that the range of clinical actions, techniques, and protocols are organized around the use, insertion, management, and care of the nasogastric tube.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1507
retrieved_docs.content: DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES is a broad category that comprises several specific disorders. It includes مرض السكري دون مضاعفات, which denotes diabetes without complications, and اضطراب إفراز الجسترين, a disorder of gastrin secretion. اضطراب إفراز الجسترين is further linked to الحثل وإضطرابات الإفراز في الغشاء المخاطي المَعِدِيّ المِعَوِيّ, indicating gastric mucosal atrophy and associated secretory abnormalities. Additional conditions classified under this category are اعتلال عصبي سكري (diabetic neuropathy), ضمور نصف الوجه المترقي (progressive hemifacial atrophy), فرط أنسولين الدم (hyperinsulinemia), and ورم غلوكاغوني (glucagonoma). Understanding that these entities are subtypes or complications of endocrine pancreatic disease aids clinicians in organizing diagnostic evaluation and tailoring management strategies for patients with pancreatic endocrine dysfunction.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1452
retrieved_docs.content: DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES is a broad category that encompasses several specific conditions. Among its subentities are اعتلال عصبي سكري (diabetic neuropathy), a chronic complication in which prolonged hyperglycemia leads to damage of peripheral nerves and results in sensory loss, pain, or autonomic dysfunction. The category also includes فرط أنسولين الدم (hyperinsulinemia), a state of elevated insulin levels in the bloodstream that can arise from insulin resistance, early type 2 diabetes, or insulin‑secreting tumors and contributes to metabolic disturbances such as hypoglycemia and weight gain. Another child condition is ورم غلوكاغوني (glucagonoma), a rare neoplasm of the pancreatic α‑cells that secretes excess glucagon, producing a characteristic syndrome of hyperglycemia, necrolytic migratory erythema, weight loss, and anemia. Finally, the category lists ضمور نصف الوجه المترقي (progressive hemifacial atrophy), an uncommon, often autoimmune‑mediated disorder characterized by gradual wasting of the skin, subcutaneous tissue, and muscle on one side of the face; its inclusion reflects the recognition that endocrine pancreatic diseases, particularly those with autoimmune components, may be associated with extra‑pancreatic manifestations. Together, these entities illustrate the diverse clinical spectrum—from metabolic and neuropathic complications to neoplastic and autoimmune manifestations—linked to diseases of the endocrine pancreas, underscoring the importance of comprehensive evaluation and multidisciplinary management in affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2239
retrieved_docs.content: Hemorrhage into nerve trunk, inflammatory and toxic neuropathy, and hereditary sensory neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب الحسي الوراثي) are all conditions in which the nerve serves as the primary site of pathological findings. This highlights that the nerve is a common locus for diverse neuropathic processes, ranging from acute hemorrhagic injury to inflammatory, toxic, and genetic sensory neuropathies. Recognizing the nerve as the affected structure helps clinicians focus diagnostic evaluation and tailor management strategies for patients presenting with these nerve‑related disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 1 -- Community 204
retrieved_docs.content: pancreatic cancer. Metastatic carcinoma to a lymph node of the head and neck and metastatic squamous cell carcinoma of lymph nodes of the head and neck are both characterized as cancerous tumors; the latter is an inverse ISA of squamous cell carcinoma of the head and neck (سرطان الخلايا الحرشفية في الرأس والعنق) and carries the specific morphology of metastatic squamous cell carcinoma (سرطانة الخلايا الحرشفية النقيلية). The non‑hematologic malignant diseases group (الأمراض غير الدموية الخبيثة (SMQ)) functions as a container for secondary malignant tumors in other specified sites, malignant tumors of the upper jaw (ورم خبيث في الفك العلوي), and malignant tumor of the sublingual gland, and it is itself a member of the broader metastatic cancer category (السرطان النقيلي). This SMQ group is also listed as a member of both pancreatic cancer and melanomas of the skin, indicating that these disease families belong to the non‑hematologic malignant disease spectrum. Bone cancer (سرطان العظم)

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 1 -- Community 225
retrieved_docs.content: neoplasm to sublingual gland, Metastatic malignant neoplasm to lymph nodes of neck, Metastatic carcinoma to lymph node of head and neck, Metastatic adenocarcinoma to lymph node of head and neck, and Metastatic squamous cell carcinoma of lymph nodes of head and neck—is mapped to secondary malignancies, carries the relationship “النقيلة” (metastasis), and is defined by inverse‑isa relationships to site‑specific terms such as “نقائل إلى الرقبة” (metastasis to the neck) or “الورم السرطاني” (cancerous tumor). The non‑hematologic malignant diseases grouping (الأمراض غير الدموية الخبيثة (SMQ)) includes the subgroup “نقائل إلى الرقبة,” indicating that cervical lymph‑node metastases are recognized as a distinct member of this disease group. This SMQ also contains the entity “Neoplasm, uncertain whether benign or malignant,” which carries the “النقيلة” relationship, reflecting its potential for metastatic behavior. Cancer of pancreas is a member of the SMQ, reinforcing its placement among
